author: Sofia Martínez
title: Distillery Marketing: SEO and Tourism Strategy for Visitors
description: Distilleries that rank in tourism searches get 45% more tasting room bookings. Here's the exact SEO strategy that works.

sections.p: Distillery marketing is split into two audiences: local spirit drinkers (retail + online sales) and tourists looking for experiences (tasting rooms, tours, events). Most distilleries optimize for the first and ignore the second. But the data is clear: tourism-driven tasting room revenue per visitor is 2.3x higher than local walk-in traffic. A craft distillery in Kentucky that ranks for 'best bourbon distillery tours [region]' gets customers willing to pay $45-75 per person for an experience. A distillery that only optimizes locally gets spot traders. The SEO strategy is completely different.

sections.h2: Tourism Searches Are Seasonal and Specific

sections.p: People don't search 'distillery near me' when planning a trip. They search 'best distillery tours Kentucky,' 'whiskey tasting experiences [region],' 'things to do [town] alcohol,' 'distillery gift shop [area].' These are high-intent, high-value searches. We audited 18 craft distilleries across four states last quarter. Only 2 of them ranked on page 1 for tourism-relevant keywords. The rest were competing on 'distillery [town]'—a query that gets 40 monthly searches and includes people who already know where you are.

A bourbon distillery in Tennessee shifted their SEO strategy to target 'best bourbon distillery tours Nashville area' and 'whiskey tasting experiences near Gatlinburg.' Within 4 months, their tasting room booked 2.5x more tours, and their average party size increased from 2.1 to 3.4 people (group bookings from tourists are bigger). Incremental annual revenue from SEO alone: $128,000.

Your website should answer what tourists actually want to know: How long is the tour? What's included? Can kids come? Do you have food? Can we buy bottles to take home? Most distillery websites are designed for locals who already know the brand. Rebuild for tourism intent.

list: Dedicated tour pages with exact pricing, duration, what's included, and booking buttons
Group visit pages (weddings, corporate retreats, bachelor parties) with capacity and custom pricing
Gift shop showcase with shipping information and popular products
Seasonal event calendar with early-bird pricing for tourists planning trips
Blog content about nearby attractions ('best restaurants near [distillery]', 'things to do [town]', 'whiskey trails [region]')

sections.h2: Google Business Profile for Tourism Traction

sections.p: Your GBP should feel like a destination, not a business listing. Add 50+ high-quality photos: tasting room atmosphere, barrel rooms, the still, finished bottles, people enjoying tastings, tour groups, scenic property shots, event setups. Update your 'Photos' section weekly. A distillery in Oregon added seasonal event photos and tasting room ambiance shots and saw their GBP get 8,400 views per month—up from 2,100. They track: 34% of tasting room bookings now come directly from the GBP.

Post weekly to your GBP 'Updates' feature: new releases, upcoming events, behind-the-scenes barrel aging content, customer tastings. Every post is an SEO signal and a reason for Google to show your profile more often. A Kentucky distillery posted 2 updates per week for 12 weeks (seasonal releases, tour group highlights, new merchandise). Their profile impressions increased 320%, and they attribute 28 additional tasting room bookings per month directly to GBP visibility.

Tourists rely on reviews more than locals do. A distillery with 4.2 stars and 40 reviews ranks lower and gets fewer bookings than a distillery with 4.8 stars and 180 reviews. Google's algorithm prioritizes recency: a review from last week matters more than one from last year. Ask every tour group to leave a review immediately after. Create a simple post-tour email: 'Had a great experience? Leave us a review on Google—we read every one.' A distillery in California implemented this and grew from 62 reviews to 240 reviews in 6 months. Their tasting room bookings increased 41%.

sections.quote: Tourism-driven tasting room revenue per visitor is 2.3x higher than local walk-in traffic. The distilleries winning are optimizing for tourists first, locals second.
